data.title: Holding POET Into Q4 Earnings: The Numbers That Matter
data.description:
Introduction

The Canadian company Poet Technologies Inc. (POET) is in the midst of the transition from a pre-revenue stage to operational expansion and commercialization of its photonic integrated circuits (PICs) and optical modules (800G and 1.6T, with the 3.2T in the development stage), protected by patents. As I explained, its main innovation is the Optical Interposer, which integrates photonic and electronic components on a single chip without wires. The advantages of this solution are lower cost (fewer components are needed) and power efficiency. We immediately understand that we are talking about the intriguing layer of the AI stack. Compute is where NVIDIA and AMD play, while we find POET in the infrastructure and networking layers. This means that POET is not a computer or a software company. Its optical engines move data between AI accelerators at extremely fast speeds (basically, the speed of light). Without this role, GPUs would be a massive bottleneck for AI. With an analogy taken from the human body, NVIDIA plays the role of the brain, while POET produces components that are part of the nervous system.

After developing the technology, POET has secured manufacturing capacity through Globetronics and NationGate in Malaysia. The company also has a partnership with Mitsubishi, Foxconn, and Luxshare, which are suppliers of the hyperscalers.

It may sound obvious, but if POET manages to execute, it could be a real beneficiary of the ongoing data center buildout, which is expected to last, at least, through 2030. As POET shows, the inflection point for 800G demand is already behind us, but 2026 is the ramp year for 1.6T, which should soon see its TAM grow to $1.5B in two years, with the $15B landmark in sight by 2030. To sum it all up, the TAM that POET estimated in its investor presentation has already been revised by 43% for 2026 and by 46% for 2027.

A big variable for POET is CPO (co-packaged optics) growth, which seeks to eliminate pluggable modules from the switch chassis. Now, on one side, POET actually serves the pluggable market, which means that CPO growth could cannibalize it, especially if the hyperscalers decide to skip the 1.6T modules and go to the CPO switches from NVIDIA or Broadcom. But this is a short-term issue because, over the long term, POET's light source products are designed for the architecture that CPO uses: chip-to-chip optical connectivity inside the servers. Since the CPO market is growing extremely fast, many believe that this, per se, validates the fact that POET is trying to solve a real issue and service an explosive market. The issue here is timing. So far, in this field, POET is at the prototype stage, and POET needs demand for 1.6T pluggable modules to hold and grow. If this doesn't happen, then the company may find itself caught in a dangerous gap.

It is hard to predict what will happen, but I think that the data center buildout is such a massive trend in a world where hardware suppliers are more and more constrained that we will see multiple technologies used as long as they are ready for use (by the way, this could lead us to those considerations about the real lifecycle of the AI capex and how it should be properly amortized).

POET's Q4 FY25 Earnings Preview

Before we dive into crunching a few numbers, let me give you a piece of context, in case you are not familiar with the financials of this company. Since we are at a pre-revenue stage, POET's income statement is basically non-existent, as it is more a statement of operations. Therefore, we don't have to focus on the bottom line but on the operating loss and the operating cash. As of Q3 FY25, the reported revenue was $300k, the net loss was $9.4M, but the operating loss was $2.8M.

What stands out is this: revenues have been increasing for five consecutive quarters; the cash burn is improving from where it was a year ago, at around $9M.

Having said that, let's move on to the upcoming Q4 print. The pivotal question, as far as I see it, is whether any product revenue will appear and be recognized. The revenue that the consensus expects is $500k, which may suggest that some initial production may appear. In fact, we know that POET secured a $500k order for 1.6T modules in September 2025, which should have already been delivered in the first months of 2026. Secondly, in October 2025, the company received a $5M order to be shipped in H2 2026. What matters is the first one, and in case we see some product revenue, I believe investors will celebrate. Otherwise, we will have to wait for the Q1 FY26 print, confirming that 2026 will be a make-or-break year, at least for the stock's performance.

If product revenue appears, we can then see for the first time what the company's gross margin looks like. This is of utmost importance because POET has long claimed its competitive cost advantage over other solutions but has not yet given us real numbers to prove it.

Finally, we will need to pay attention to how the CEO will speak about 2026. So far, this year has been described as the revenue ramp year. In this regard, the upcoming report is even more important because, being released at the end of March, it could also shed more light on what has been going on in the first three months of this pivotal year. If we hear talks about shipment schedules and new orders, then we could say that the company has definitely exited the pre-revenue stage.

Valuation

Clearly, when we lack the main financial figures, it is really hard to value the company, as most multiples are either undefined or don't make a lot of sense (for example, the fwd P/sales is above 700). An investor who chooses to buy POET is not seeking earnings power, since the stock, right now, seems closer to an option with a certain degree of probability that the outcome will be in favor of the buyer.

Right now, POET trades at around $6.00, which leads to a market cap of roughly $877M. As a result of a cash position of $92M and total debt below $8M, we have an EV of $791M. This means that the company would need to report $79M in revenue just to lead to a 10x EV/revenue multiple, which is a scenario that not even the bulls imagine. I think it would be a great result if POET ended this year with $10M in revenue. This means the multiple is actually 79.

Let me dig a bit deeper and explain my $10M forecast. In 2026, 1.6T transceiver sales begin, and we said that this could lead to sales of $15B by 2030. POET sells optical engines, which represent a component worth around 20% to 30% of the transceiver value. This means that the TAM for POET could be worth between $3B and $4.5B at the 2030 peak. The revenue, I imagine, means that this year, POET will have a market share of around 0.25%. It is surely not an aggressive hypothesis, but to reach revenues of almost $80M, we would need POET to have a market share above 2%. It is possible, but POET needs the scale, which is probably not the story for 2026 but, more likely, for 2027 through 2030. In this assumption, I consider that light sources are still a long-term project that doesn't have an impact on 2026 revenues.

Conclusion

I think that, compared to a quarter ago, the outlook has improved a bit, and this is why I am upgrading the stock from sell to hold. But I remain on the sidelines because the outcome of this investment relies heavily on how the company will execute. My rule is to let some time pass to test how the company operates. I know I may leave some money on the table, but I have also seen that once one identifies a winner correctly, there is plenty of upside still left.

data.title: Super Micro Computer Faces Immense Reputational Risk
data.description:
Super Micro Computer (SMCI) can’t catch a break.

Following years of highly publicized accounting slip-ups, the company now finds itself in another regulatory quagmire, this time pertaining to the sale of NVIDIA (NVDA) chips to China. Although the company itself has not been accused of any wrongdoing, several of its top executives have been implicated in sales that–if real–are contrary to U.S. regulations. Co-founder Yih-Shyan Liaw was among those named in documents filed by regulators. Liaw’s high profile poses the threat of reputational damage to Super Micro, as Liaw was a decision maker serving on the company's board until a few days ago.

This isn’t the first time that Super Micro Computer has made headlines for questionable reasons. Prior to the NVIDIA controversy, the company was famous for accounting issues, including auditor resignations, delayed statements, and missed deadlines. These issues were severe enough that the company’s stock plunged over the last 12 months despite reporting extremely impressive growth numbers. It’s not all that often that you see a company growing the top line by 34% trading at just 12 times earnings, but when regulatory risk becomes elevated, you can get some strange outcomes.

So, Super Micro Computer investors face a complex situation. The company’s growth numbers are very good; its valuation is cheap on a sector-relative basis; and its margins are respectable. If all of this were “legitimate” then it would be an open and shut case: a slam dunk buy. The problem is twofold:

  1. The company's financials might not be reliable. This is the crux of the problem. Credible reports from the highest levels of the U.S. government have said that the GPU sales now being investigated, were facilitated by unreliable documents and possible deceptions of inspectors. While this matter is technically distinct from the accounting irregularities that were the concern last year, it raises similar questions: unreliable documents and denial of access to key inspectors, are both seen as possible signs of misstatement. This, combined with the previous resignation of SMCI's auditor, casts doubt on the notion that the company's accounting issues have been fully resolved.

  2. There is credible basis for thinking that top SMCI officers are going to be under regulatory scrutiny. This is already the case with co-founder Liaw, who was both a board member and Vice President of Business Development before his recent resignation.

So, while Super Micro Computer stock looks cheap right now, it may be a value trap, particularly if the accounting issues surface again. Speaking of which: the ongoing NVIDIA controversy ties in with the accounting concerns that surfaced in years prior. While technically no new accounting issues have surfaced this year--the last news on the matter having been a clean audit opinion last year--the NVIDIA story casts doubt on whether we’ve heard the last we’ll ever hear on the matter. Audit and regulatory compliance both fall under the category of “corporate governance.” They are closely related; as mentioned previously, some of the methods allegedly used in the GPU sales operation, were similar to known predictors of accounting misstatement.

When I last covered Super Micro Computer, I rated the stock a sell, mainly because I thought that the company’s accounting issues raised too many red flags. Since then, the stock has declined 42% in price, underperforming the S&P 500 by roughly 50%. My previous rating was a clear success. In fact, had the “on the ground” situation with SMCI remained unchanged from last year, I’d probably be declaring victory and upgrading SMCI to hold by now. However, the re-emergence of regulatory and governance issues at the company again raises the level of risk. Accordingly, I will be maintaining my SMCI rating at sell.

The Latest Controversy

The details on Super Micro’s latest controversy have been well covered on Seeking Alpha and elsewhere. To summarize:

  • The U.S. Department of Justice [DoJ] said that three individuals connected to SMCI had helped sell NVIDIA chips to China.

  • Those chips were not to be sold to China under U.S. regulations that prevailed at the time, unless the seller had a special license.

  • Neither Super Micro nor the three persons of interest had the required license.

  • Up to $2.5 billion dollars worth of GPUs were said to have been shipped under questionable circumstances.

The transactions above come after years of concerns about financial reporting quality at Super Micro, which I covered extensively in my last article on the stock. Although the current controversy is technically a new and distinct matter, it along with the previous accounting controversy raise questions about Super Micro’s corporate governance practices. With severe enough governance issues, it can get to a point where investors start doubting a company’s financial statements. Under these circumstances, most financial ratios you could use to evaluate a company’s performance become unreliable, since the data used to calculate them is in question. That, I would argue, is where we are with Super Micro today.

Future Prospects

Having looked at where things stand with Super Micro today, we can now attempt to evaluate where the company is heading. This is the hard part, as we’re talking about a company facing significant reputational risk. Theoretically, Super Micro should be a growth company, as it operates in the high growth computer storage/server industry. Going by the company's NAICS code alone, we’d expect SMCI to keep doing high growth into the future. However, there are so many red flags around this company, that you have to wonder whether clients are going to start cancelling orders, backing out of contracts, or seeking alternative suppliers. Chinese AI/computer chip matters are very sensitive for the U.S. government, frequently being discussed in terms of national security. The reputational risks here are extremely serious. For this reason, I wouldn’t assume any positive growth from SMCI in the near future.

Valuation

Having explored recent developments at SMCI as well as its future prospects, we can now look at the stock’s multiples. This part of the analysis looks fairly bullish at first–the stock is optically dirt cheap going by tech sector standards–but as we’ll see shortly, there are some major caveats here.

As the image above shows, SMCI scores an “A” on valuation from Seeking Alpha Quant, with almost all of its multiples being far below the tech sector averages. The P/E ratio of 12.26 translates to an earnings yield of 8.15%--nearly double the current 10 year treasury yield. That fact on the surface of things argues that SMCI is worth the investment.

There’s just one problem: Super Micro is a far riskier than average company, facing elevated reputational risk as well as on-again off-again financial reporting quality concerns. The company is currently embroiled in a controversy that touches on one of the most hot button issues in U.S. politics today--tech transfers to China--and had financial reporting issues severe enough to earn it a non-compliance notice from NASDAQ (NDAQ) at one point. The risk here is significant.

One way to handle elevated risk in a discounted cash flow [DCF] model is to add a very high risk premium to the company’s discount rate, which is the long term treasury yield plus a risk premium. The 10 year treasury was yielding 4.33% at the time of this writing. 6% is considered a “very high” risk premium. So, a 10.33% discount rate seems fair to use here.

Super Micro Computer had $0.74 worth of free cash flow [FCF] per share in the TTM period. That divided by 10.33% equals $7.18. I won’t assume any growth here, because SMCI faces reputation risk that could disrupt its previously high growth rates. So I will conclude that SMCI is not worth more than $10, and is a sell at today’s price of $22.23.

Risks

While I think I’ve made a compelling case that Super Micro Computer is a very risky stock, those shorting the stock face risks as well. These include:

  • A major management shakeup. Most of SMCI’s current issues have to do with management behavior. A major management shakeup could therefore potentially lead to a sudden price spike, particularly if the new managers had good reputations.

  • Bullish industry news. Despite SMCI’s company-specific issues, the firm’s industry is a strong one, expected to grow at 14.8% CAGR to 2033. Bullish news stories about the server industry could trigger quick, sharp appreciation in SMCI shares, putting highly levered shorts at risk.

The Bottom Line

While it has some admitted strengths, Super Micro Computer overall looks like a mountain of red flags today. The company’s reputation is very much at risk. Over time, reputational damage could eat away at its top-line growth, leading to negative earnings impacts. For this reason, I consider SMCI stock a sell, despite its optical cheapness.

data.title: Chatham Lodging Trust: Well-Run And Substantially Undervalued
data.description:
Introduction

I have written about several hospitality REITs previously, most recently on Services Property Trust (SVC) and Host Hotels & Resorts (HST). In terms of size, the focus of today’s article, Chatham Lodging Trust (CLDT) is similar to the former. Chatham Lodging Trust is a small-cap hospitality REIT which presents a compelling opportunity to investors looking for a well-run REIT with room for substantial growth.

Portfolio

Chatham Lodging Trust’s portfolio of hotels are in two main categories: upscale extended-stay hotels and premium-branded select-service hotels. Following the acquisition of six Hilton-branded hotels earlier this month, the company’s portfolio comprises 39 hotels with approximately 5,610 rooms. However, its main focus is on the extended-stay segment, with 64% of the company’s total room count falling under this category. This same category also accounted for 66% of the company’s EBITDA.

Compared to other hotels, extended-stay hotels typically outperform in economic downturns. This is due to several reasons, such as lower labor costs (a benefit shared by select-service hotels) and longer average stays, all of which mean the company has better margins. In fact, the company’s EBITDA margins are one of the highest, if not the highest, in the industry.

Balance Sheet

One of the advantages Chatham Lodging Trust has comes in the form of its balance sheet. As of 31 December 2025, the company’s total debt stands at $343 million, at a weighted average rate of 6.2%, comprising $143 million in fixed-rate mortgage debt and $200 million on an unsecured term loan. In terms of liquidity, the company has $324 million in total liquidity, comprising $24 million of unrestricted cash and a further $300 million available to it from its revolving credit facility. Its net debt to EBITDA stands at a multiple of 3.4x, one of the lowest among its peers in the industry. Crucially, on top of having low debt, its debt is also spread out fairly widely. No debt is due this year and the next, and only $48 million is due in the subsequent 2 years.

The company’s strong balance sheet has not come about by accident. Management has made a conscious effort to reduce the company’s debt and has been able to reduce its net debt by $457 million, or 59%, over the past 5 years, from 31 March 2020 to 31 December 2025.

The company’s strong balance sheet provides management with substantial strategic flexibility, positioning the company well to withstand any economic downturns, such as during the COVID pandemic, or take advantage of any opportunities in the market.

Dividends & Share Buybacks

Earlier this month, the company announced a quarterly dividend of $0.10/share, an 11% increase on its previous quarterly dividend of $0.09/share and marking its second consecutive year of double-digit dividend growth. The previous increase came a year ago, when the company increased its quarterly dividends from $0.07/share to $0.09/share, a 29% increase. At an annualized dividend of $0.40/share, the forward dividend yield based on the current share price of $7.92/share is approximately 5%. This dividend is comfortably covered by its Adjusted Funds From Operations (AFFO), at around 36.7% of its 2026 midpoint AFFO guidance of $1.09/share. This low payout ratio not only indicates the possibility of future dividend increases, it also provides the company with substantial free cash flow for future acquisitions and continued share repurchases.

In 2025, the company repurchased 1.8 million shares, approximately 4% of outstanding shares, at an average price of $6.87/share for a total of $12.6 million. The company still has around $13 million available under its share buyback program. Of note would be the fact that management has highlighted that $10 million of share repurchases at current prices would be 2% accretive to the company’s AFFO/share.

Valuation

Based on its current share price of $7.92/share, the Chatham Lodging Trust is currently trading at just over 7.2x the midpoint of its 2026 AFFO guidance of $1.09/share. This compares very favorably to its peers, which are trading at a forward P/AFFO multiple of around 14x. Even taking a conservative 10x multiple would see the company trade at close to $11/share, representing a 38.9% increase on current share prices. Moreover, the company is currently trading at a price-to-book ratio (P/B ratio) of 0.49, one of the lowest it has been in recent years.

While there isn’t much coverage on the company, given its size, it is worth noting that the few analysts covering the stock have either a “Hold” or “Buy” rating. Additionally, their average price target of $9.33/share is substantially higher than the current share price, representing close to 18% upside.

All things considered, the company is undervalued, both relative to its peers and to the book value of its properties.

2026 Guidance and Catalysts

While it is evident that Chatham Lodging Trust is a well-run company with industry-leading margins and a strong balance sheet, there are a couple of other catalysts that I believe provide significant upside for the company.

Firstly, market weakness in the company’s Silicon Valley and Bellevue areas has compressed its reported earnings far below their intrinsic earnings power. The 5 properties in these markets contributed 25% of the company’s EBITDA in 2019, its largest concentration. However, the $35 million in EBITDA generated in 2019 has declined significantly by almost 40% to $21.5 million in 2025. This is largely driven by a decline in business travel to the Bay Area.

While this may be a potential area of concern, it does also represent a significant opportunity for the company. According to management, just a 50% recovery of the difference would add $7 million in EBITDA, or $0.13/share in incremental FFO. A full recovery back to 2019 levels would see $0.27/share in FFO, representing a 24% increase relative to the 2026 midpoint guidance.

While this may sound very optimistic, the current macroeconomic environment is ideal for the company. Substantial investment flowing into artificial intelligence data centers and semiconductor fabrication will generate substantial demand for the company’s properties. CEO Jeffrey Fisher said as much in a recent announcement: “The combination of historically low new supply growth, record amounts of new investments in technology, especially with respect to artificial intelligence, and reshoring manufacturing back into the United States should result in strong, multi-year growth for the lodging industry.” As long as the company’s Silicon Valley and Bellevue markets see a slight recovery in the next couple of years, the impact to the company’s earnings would be substantial.

Another catalyst comes in the form of the company’s acquisition of the six Hilton-branded hotels mentioned above. These hotels were acquired at a 10% capitalization rate and are superior to the older hotels the company has sold over the past 18 months in terms of margins, age of hotels, and RevPAR, thereby enhancing the company’s portfolio.

Moreover, management expects the acquisition to be accretive to the FFO by $0.10/share. Crucially, as the acquisition was announced after the company’s Q4 2025 earnings, the 2026 guidance does not include this $0.10/share.

Potential Risks

As always, there are several risks to consider. Hospitality REITs are exceptionally susceptible to the macroeconomic environment, and any prolonged downturn in the economy will inevitably affect booking rates. While the company is somewhat insulated due to its focus on extended-stay hotels, it will still see a decline in revenue.

Another potential risk comes in the form of the company’s size, causing small-cap liquidity risk. Due to its relatively small market capitalization (around $370 million), this means the company’s shares are susceptible to high volatility, as one large sale can cause its share price to tank. This volatility is inherent to small-cap stocks and not specific to the company, but it is something investors should note of.

Conclusion

Chatham Lodging Trust is a well-run REIT. With its focus on the extended-stay segment, the company has achieved industry leading EBITDA margins over the years and boasts a very healthy balance sheet. The company also pays a very decent dividend of 5%, and has shown a track record of increasing its dividends. Moreover, its current dividend is well-covered and also provides room for potential increases.

Despite all these positives, the company is currently at less than half its book value. In fact, by almost any measure, it is trading substantially lower than that of its peers. Additionally, there are a couple of catalysts that I believe could cause the company to outperform in the next couple of years. Hence, given the significant undervaluation and potential for outperformance this year, I believe there is a sufficient margin of safety for the company. Meanwhile, I will be collecting a steady 5% dividend yield. All things considered, I rate the company a “Buy”.

data.title: Palo Alto Networks: AI Threat Is Real
data.description:
Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (PANW), has slumped back towards the yearly lows due to the AI threat. Even if AI doesn't replace cybersecurity, the threat will continue hitting the valuation multiples of the stock. My investment thesis remains Bearish on the stock due to its expensive valuation compared to the organic growth rate of the platformization business.

AI Threat

Palo Alto Networks fell 5% on Databricks announcing the entry into the security information and event management, or SIEM, category with the help of Anthropic (ANTHRO). The AI data company will utilize Anthropic to help monitor data with the new Lakewatch product.

On top of this threat, Palo Alto Networks recently spent $25 billion on buying CyberArk and another $3.35 billion on Chronosphere. These moves signal the platformization push has multiple layers of holes.

Palo Alto Networks is a big player in SIEM, with the company shifting terminology to XSIAM, or extended security intelligence and automation management. The company now has over 600 XSIAM customers, with customers actively using AgentiX to deploy agents to monitor infrastructure for security.

The cybersecurity company is an overall powerhouse, but the concept of Databricks already having security customers like Anthropic and Adobe Systems (ADBE) questions the whole platformization concept. As pointed out with the CyberArk deal to enter the identity space, the platforms are only as good as the latest tech, leading to questions on whether customers want best-of-breed point products or a platform that is tightly integrated.

Palo Alto Networks reported FQ2 total platformization reached ~1,550, up about 400 YoY for around 35% growth. Based on this metric, the platformization concept appears to be working.

Hidden Weakness

The company reported FQ2 '26 revenue of $2.6 billion, which only grew at a 14.6% clip. Palo Alto Networks has struggled with returning to exceptional growth ever since incorporating the platformization concept, in part due to the costs and time required for large customers to transition a whole platform worth of cybersecurity systems from legacy products.

Going forward, the market has to parse quarterly results to focus on organic growth with the addition of 2 large acquisitions. CyberArk alone added $1.36 billion worth of annual revenues and ARR of $1.44 billion.

Palo Alto Networks guided to FQ3 revenues of $2.93 billion for up to 29% growth. The FY26 guidance was for revenues of $11.3 billion for up to 23% growth, with about $760 million worth of revenues from the M&A deals.

The CyberArk deal adds the majority of the $340 million added to the FQ3 revenues after the deal closed on February 11. The deal doesn't fully count all of the April quarter revenue and costs Palo Alto Networks $45 in cash per share and 2.2005 shares of the stock.

The company is only guiding to ~$2.6 billion for FQ3 on an organic basis for only about 13% growth on the $2.29 billion reported back in the same period for FY25. In no surprise, the legacy Palo Alto business shows signs of slowing down heading into the 2 big deals.

Palo Alto will pay out $4.9 billion in cash to close the 2 deals, with $2.6 billion for Chromosphere and another $2.3 billion for CyberArk. The company will have a pro forma cash balance of $5.6 billion before incorporating the cash balances and debt from the merged entities, with CyberArk having ~$800 million in net cash.

As with any company with major acquisitions, valuations require a look at clean numbers with the numbers fully integrated into the consensus analyst estimates. Palo Alto starts the new FY in August, and analysts forecast an FY27 EPS of $3.97 based on the updated financials with the 2 acquisitions and the related share dilution.

The stock trades at 41x EPS targets for FY27, with analysts only forecasting sub-10% EPS growth. Analysts are forecasting EPS growth returns to the 15% range in future years, so PANW stock trades at more than 2x EPS growth rates and nearly a huge premium of 3x growth rates.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that the AI threat is at least going to push Palo Alto Networks down to a more reasonable valuation after trading at a huge premium for years. The stock would trade at close to $120 based on even just 30x EPS targets for FY27 versus the current price of $157.

Investors should let Palo Alto Networks continue to fall before reaching attractive levels. Remember, though, the $120 price level is the valuation target, not a level at which an investor would expect a solid return for the AI risk.

data.title: LTPZ: From Perfect Storm To Opportunity
data.description:
The PIMCO 15+ Year U.S. TIPS Index Exchange-Traded Fund (LTPZ) has suffered collateral damage from the Iran war, falling back to its lowest level since mid-2025. In doing so its real expected yield has risen to 2.7%, offering a great opportunity for investors willing to take on the risk of high volatility due to its sensitivity to interest rate expectations. The economy and US government in particular cannot sustain such elevated real yields and it is likely a matter of time before the Fed steps in to lower them. The falling bond yields and rising inflation expectations that would likely result could easily see the LTPZ double in value.

Rising Inflation A Double Edge Sword

The LTPZ holds a portfolio of inflation linked bonds with maturities of 15 years and above, with an average effective maturity of 21.8 years and an effective duration of 19.6 years. This makes the ETF extremely sensitive to changes in long term rate expectations, which can be seen in the chart below where the LTPZ is shown alongside 30-year inflation-linked bond yields. It currently offers a yield of 2.7% less its 0.2% expense fee, plus the reported inflation rate, meaning that in theory rising inflation benefit the ETF. Over the long term the LTPZ should perform almost identically to similar maturity regular bonds such as the ones held by the iShares 20+ Year Treasury ETF (TLT) if actual reported inflation comes in in line with current expectations of 2.2%. If inflation averages higher, the LTPZ will outperform, but it will still return 2.7% real if held to maturity. It is also worth noting that the LTPZ is significantly less liquid than the TLT.

When inflation expectations rise as they have in recent weeks following the oil price spike, the LTPZ tends to outperform the TLT as investors chase inflation linked bonds driving their yields down. Typically, a 1% rise in long term inflation expectations leads to around a 15% rise in the LTPZ relative to the TLT.

So why has the LTPZ performed so poorly despite rising oil prices? There are three reasons explaining recent weakness.

Contained Inflation Expectations

Firstly, bond markets only see the rise in oil prices impacting inflation over the next few years not the next few decades. As seen below, while 5-year inflation expectations have risen sharply, 30-year expectations have actually edged lower.

Rising Rate Expectations

Secondly, investors have sold bonds as they expect rising oil prices to cause the Fed to be more hawkish, which has seen the entire rate curve move higher. This can be seen in the chart of the expected short term rate over the next 10 years.

Rising Term Premium

Thirdly, rising geopolitical and fiscal uncertainty has caused long term yields to spike in excess of where rate expectations would suggest as they seek greater compensation for taking on long duration assets.

The Fed Will Have To Bring Yields Down

When we add these factors together, long-term inflation-linked bond yields have risen by around 30bps, causing a decline in the LTPZ of over 5%. Real yields of 2.7% are now far too high for the economy and government to sustain. The only options to prevent a debt spiral for the US government is for real borrowing costs to fall below the rate of real GDP or to run primary budget surpluses. As the latter seems extremely unlikely and real GDP growth is trending around 2%, the Fed will have to intervene in the market to bring real yields down if the markets don't do it on their own.

While the LTPZ is currently selling off in part due to concerns over the impact of the war on US fiscal accounts, it is the rise in debt that will ultimately drive the its recovery. In my view, when the next recession hits, rising bond issuance will force the Fed to resume its bond purchases, which will put a cap on yields and drive up inflation expectations.

Asymmetric Upside Risks

My core view it that long-term real yields will fall back to zero, and the last time this was the case the LTPZ traded 70% higher than current levels. A move down to -0.5% as seen in 2021 could see the LTPZ double. There is of course a risk that yields rise further before their weight on the economy and Treasury forces a reversal. The record high real yield for long-term US TIPS was 3.2% seen in 2008 and a return to those levels would suggest around 10% downside for the LTPZ based on current duration. Any further yield increase beyond here are not only highly unlikely but would also occur alongside falling duration and higher dividends, meaning total return losses should remain moderate even under a worst case scenario.

Summary

Due to its high duration the LTPZ is heavily exposed to changes in long term interest rate expectations, which have risen lately as rising oil prices have led to expectations of tighter Fed policy. It has also suffered from the rise in the term premium which appears linked to uncertainty about the US fiscal position. However, this mini crisis has generated another great entry point for investors who are willing to accept near term risk for significant upside potential. The factors impacting the LTPZ in recent weeks strengthen the case for Fed intervention that should drive down real yields and cause significant long term returns.

data.title: Lennar's Margin Pressures Persist
data.description:
Shares of Lennar Corporation (LEN) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 20% of their value. The housing market has remained persistently sluggish, and the company’s margins have suffered as it has maintained overly aggressive delivery targets, in my view. The recent rise in interest rates has also further dampened hopes of a potential spring recovery in sales. I last covered Lennar in December, downgrading shares to a “Sell,” and that call has played out with the stock losing 27% since then. Given such underperformance and with updated financials, now is a good time to revisit Lennar.

Macro Environment Remains Difficult

Over the past year, mortgage rates had steadily declined as long-term yields settled down and markets priced in several rate cuts. As we entered 2026, there was hope we could see rates sustainably below 6%, a key level that could bring more demand into the market. The decline from ~7% to 6.25% admittedly did little to strengthen the market, given just how unaffordable housing became—about 79% above pre-COVID levels. That said, a drop below 6%, in my view, would bring in some incremental buyers and at least stabilize pricing in Sun Belt markets.

More recently, we have seen rates tick back up, approaching 6.3%, as long-term yields have risen in response to the Iran War. We likely need to see the 10-year Treasury consistently below 4% for mortgage rates to stay below 6%. With increased inflation and issuance needs, that appears less likely, and I now expect no more than one Fed rate cut this year. This rise in rates comes just as we are entering the key spring selling season, further undermining hopes for a recovery. I expect transaction volumes to be flattish this year, and I continue to see national prices declining 0%-3%, with bigger declines in Sun Belt markets that have more supply.

Weak Margins Drive A Soft Q1

These macro challenges are apparent in recent financial results. In the company’s fiscal first quarter, Lennar earned $0.93, which was $0.03 below expectations as revenue plunged over 13% to $6.6 billion. The company has also been forced to rely on incentives to maintain buyer traffic, with incentives running at 14.1% in Q1, and management described affordability as “the defining constraint.” Deliveries fell by 5% to 16,863 homes. The average selling price on a home was down $34,000 to $374k. This partly reflects its shift down-market over the past year, a strategy I am cautious of given particularly difficult affordability for first-time buyers.

Gross margins were 15.2%. This was down 350 bps over the past year, and gross profit declined by about a third to $951 million as a result. The company has been able to reduce building times by 11% over the past year, helping to reduce construction costs to offset headwinds from the weaker pricing environment. These productivity benefits cannot fully offset the weaker pricing environment, and I expect margins to remain lower YoY through 2026. Lennar primarily operates across Sun Belt markets (similar to most builders), and these markets have higher inventory levels than the Northeast, which is limiting pricing power and pressuring margins.

Reflecting the loss of operating leverage from lower sales activity, SG&A was 9.8% of revenue. This was up 130 bps from last year, even as total spending was flat at $617 million. I view its expense performance as solid, and ultimately, the level of sales is the primary challenge for margins. Beyond this, its community count is up 6% from last year, reflecting its effort to boost sales, which assumed a more favorable market environment. With ongoing sluggishness, I remain cautious that its overstretched growth will continue to weigh on margins.

Demand Is Muted

New orders were up 1% from last year, though this remains a very muted level, at 18,515. While the volume of orders was up, the value of these orders was down $300 million to $7.1 billion, reflecting the weakness of the pricing environment. Pricing was especially weak in its “Central” region, which includes Sun Belt states like the Carolinas, Tennessee, and Georgia. Lennar’s backlog now stands at $6 billion, or ~16,000 homes. This provides less than 3 months of visibility, and given its fairly thin backlog, Lennar may have to further moderate construction levels if orders do not improve.

I remain a bit cautious on the company’s inventory position. During the quarter, it built about $500 million of inventory, reaching $10.5 billion. A sequential build is to be expected during Q1 given the seasonally quiet selling season and increased activity in preparation for the spring selling season. This is up from $10.15 billion last year. Given the fact demand is not recovering in a meaningful way, I believe the company has a fairly high level of inventory. As such, we are likely to see an ongoing reliance on incentives to maintain volumes and work down inventory, capping any potential margin improvement.

Now, while the environment is difficult, Lennar maintains a strong financial position. It is carrying over $2 billion in cash and just $4 billion in debt, so debt-to-capital is below 16%. Given its solid balance sheet, I view its 2.2% dividend yield as secure. Beyond its dividend, LEN repurchased $237 million of stock in Q1. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down 7% from last year, and I expect buybacks to continue at a similar pace.

Shares Have Further Downside Given Falling Earnings

Looking forward, the company aims to deliver 20-21,000 homes in Q2 with margins expanding by at least 40 bps. It continues to target 85,000 deliveries in 2026. I see downside risk to this target unless we see mortgage rates reverse back lower, which does not appear imminent.

Ultimately, Lennar is facing a difficult environment that is weighing on homebuilders more broadly. That said, its sub-$500k price point, elevated inventories, and focus on maintaining volumes over margins have exacerbated these pressures. This drove my negative view in December and has played out, but at some point, valuation may be pricing that in. We are not there yet. Back in December, I was targeting $8.50 in EPS. However, this was far too optimistic (even as I was below consensus at the time).

Considering the challenges in the homebuilding market, I expect Lennar to earn $5.00-$6.00 this year on 80,000-85,000 deliveries. This assumes mortgage rates remain above 6% on average and unemployment is broadly stable from here. That leaves shares at ~16x earnings. While 2026 may well be the trough year, I do not see a substantial acceleration in housing activity. Given that and the cyclicality of the business, I struggle to pay more than 12-14x, meaning I see a fair value of no more than $80. With another ~15% downside even after significant underperformance, I remain a “Sell” on Lennar and would continue to avoid shares.

data.title: PDD Holdings: Temu Pessimism Creates A Deep Value Opportunity
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered PDD Holdings (PDD), I talked about the way the current pessimism about Temu's slowdown led to a significant discount on one of China's best financial fortresses, rating them a Strong Buy despite ongoing competitive pressure.

After a solid year marked by significant reinvestments and deliberate expense increases, PDD remains in an exceptional financial position, while the valuation continues to imply a significant margin of safety even when taking into account the country-related and competitive risks.

Fundamentals Remain Strong

PDD reported a weak Q4, missing the market’s top- and bottom-line estimates, with total revenues up 12% although net income dropped 12% YoY (non-GAAP) due to higher costs of revenues, marketing and R&D, alongside a ~12.3% drop in cash generated from operating activities to $15.29 billion.

This drop also comes as a result of higher investments, with R&D up 30% YoY (part of their 100-billion-yuan investment plan over three years announced in 2025), while the company’s management also highlighted 2026 as the start of their “next decade” during the Q4 Earnings Release, mentioning:

2026 marks the beginning of our journey into the next decade, (...) Going into this new chapter, supply chain investment is where we will place our greatest conviction. We are committed to dedicating significant resources, with an all-in mindset, to drive lasting benefits to the entire ecosystem.

Financially, based on PDD’s latest report, we can see not only a fortress balance sheet with meaningless debt and a ton of cash, but this is even improving, with cash and cash equivalents nearly doubling and equity up significantly, generating $3.66 billion worth of interest in 2025 from this alone.

As mentioned before, this is the “biggest” fortress in China’s Big Tech, (compared to peers like Baidu (BIDU) or JD.com (JD) which are known for their strong financials), with an exceptional financial position and potential to develop, being able to fund their expansion and even ramp it up significantly.

If they want to, we could also see PDD announce a dividend or buyback program, similar to how many of their peers have decided to return capital to shareholders in recent years, especially since their cash flow and especially the balance sheet allows it, and given the valuation, I certainly wouldn’t mind to see some buybacks alongside their significant reinvestments in their ecosystem.

Macro Weakness - But Some Improvements

Another reason why PDD jumped on the day of the release is a general boost in Chinese stocks following reports of the country moving forward with addressing unfair competition and price wars in the food-delivery industry after previously announcing probes, following a seminar held by the country's market regulator.

As a note, this was a major factor behind the stock’s crash back in Q3, when the management highlighted the “ongoing evolution of the competitive landscape and external uncertainties.”

I talked about this in previous coverage and I welcome this type of move, as these companies have indeed taken things way too far - see Meituan (MPNGF) recently announcing that they’re building a network of “rider dorms” - which can potentially improve margins and the market’s perception about the industry since it wouldn’t be a race to the bottom anymore.

Still, since we’re talking about China and a company with strong exposure to its domestic market alongside the global economy, the ongoing Iran conflict can have detrimental effects on the demand for PDD’s platforms.

Despite recent reports of Iran refuting Trump’s peace talks despite the US offering a 15-point peace plan even after that, I’ll mention again that I don’t see this as something that will last that long given its implications on the global economy and a dozen of other factors that would make the goal of a replacement way too pricey - as mentioned in more detail here.

Although this type of company should be very susceptible to a recession or a new drop in disposable budgets, they can also stand to benefit from a potential recovery, especially now that they built Temu into a globally recognised platform (regardless of quality), potentially allowing them to reinvest into marketing once budgets improve, especially since we’re talking about a true powerhouse in terms of balance sheet health.

Risks Reiterated

Despite this strong financial position and potential to expand in high-growth regions, we should keep in mind that this is ultimately a Chinese company, which comes with its own risks especially from a regulatory point of view, despite the recent developments being positive.

Meanwhile, competitive pressure remains very significant, while political uncertainty can come from far more than just China - see the previous mentions about the US closing the “de minimis” loophole for China and the EU planning to remove the €150 duty-free threshold mentioned in the previous article - potentially forcing the company into adapting.

Although I do believe in their potential especially in high-growth regions like SEA, I also mentioned before how Temu is already under significant scrutiny in several of these countries, while potential for more political/tariff uncertainty or broader macro pressure remain significant risks that can clip their wings.

Valuation

For PDD’s DCF to Equity valuation, assuming no change in net borrowings given their spotless balance sheet and potential to see a further ramp-up in growth costs (potentially even CAPEX, which are generally low), let’s assume a conservative FCF of $15 billion in 2026 as the base.

This would be followed by a CAGR of 10% (below the market’s near-term EPS estimates), backed by their investments - both the growth they can generate and their scale-down boosting the numbers - alongside potential macro improvements, followed by a more moderate 6% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 3% terminal growth rate, to take into account their potential to focus on higher-growth initiatives and regions, with a high discount rate of 13% to take into account the competitive pressure and China-related risks, as well as 1.48 billion ADRs outstanding (4:1 ADR conversion rate) as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $201.51 billion, for an intrinsic value of $136.16 per share, which is still well above the current $103.63 despite their jump on earnings day, and that’s when using a high discount rate as a conservative deterrent.

Conclusions

PDD is reiterated as a Strong Buy, as the company remains in an exceptional financial position and is focusing on reinvesting in their ecosystem, standing to benefit from several macro tailwinds.

Of course, there are many risks to keep in mind given the competitive nature of the industry and their country of origin, but the current valuation continues to imply a significant discount.

Sure, don’t expect Temu to double overnight now, and I believe growth expectations should be relatively low going forward while demanding a margin of safety that’s as high as possible, but the current price seems to be in that range already.

data.title: Micron: State Of The Cycle (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
MU Stock: FQ2 Top Line Almost Doubled YOY

I last analyzed Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) on Feb 13 with an article titled “Micron: Institutional Money Can End The Party Abruptly.” The article was promoted by activities disclosed by institutional investors and rated the stock as a Hold. Since then, there have been several notable changes surrounding the memory sector as a whole and also MU stock. The rest of this article will focus on the top one change in each category. I will detail my thoughts on A) the overall status of the current DRAM sector and B) MU’s business and growth prospects judging by the updates it provided in its recent FQ2 earnings report (ER). In the end, these changes have led me to see large odds for MU to be near a cyclical peak and caused me to downgrade my rating to Sell.

To better contextualize the rest of the discussion, let me start with a quick recap of Micron’s FQ2 2026 ER announced on Mar 18. As shown in the chart below, its reported revenue growth of 196% YOY in the quarter, demonstrating an accelerating upward trajectory over recent fiscal quarters. The quarter enjoyed a dramatic leap of sales to $23.86 billion, beating market expectations by a wide margin of $4.32 billion (i.e., a more than 22% beat).

Such an unprecedented financial expansion is largely due to both the surge in DRAM prices and also robust orders, but it nonetheless reminds me of the notoriously volatile memory cycle, as detailed next.

Long-Term DRAM Cycles

Before examining the MU tree, let’s take a look at the forest first. Historically, the long-term DRAM market has exhibited deep cyclicality with roughly a four-year period, as you can see from the following figure. During these cycles, EV/Sales ratios and contract prices move in close correlation in an extreme wild – but also quite repeatable - patterns. Historical data shows the sector EV/Sales typically peaks near the 3.2x threshold, as seen during the 2014, 2018, and 2021 cycles, while troughs often dip toward 0.7x and create a valuation contraction that is more than fourfold. Simultaneously, DRAM contract price year-over-year changes have swung violently from peaks of 120% in 2018 (and also the past 1~2 years) to troughs below negative 60% in 2023.

Currently, the sector is priced at an EV/Sales ratio of 3.18x on a TTM basis and 2.86x on an FWD basis, as seen in the second table below. Both metrics are quite close to the historical cyclical peak. As for Micron, it is trading at even higher multiples with a TTM ratio of 8.11x and an FWD ratio of 4.11x.

MU Stock’s Historical Cycles

Historically, MU has not been immune to the above cycles at all. In my experience, memory and storage hardware are among the most discretionary and commoditized items in the IT ecosystem. As a result, MU has demonstrated deep cycles – probably deeper than the industry average - in the past with roughly the same 4-year period as shown above.

As a reflection, the chart below shows its cyclical behavior in the past 10 years. The cyclical peaks and bottoms are highlighted by the red and green dashed lines, respectively.

You can see that during the expansion cycle, it enjoyed double-digit YOY growth in quarterly sales, similar to what it has enjoyed in recent quarters. During the contraction cycles, such neck-breaking growth was followed by equally rapid topline declines (ranging from about -40% to -60% YOY during the 2019 and 2023 contraction cycles). Stock prices swing widely in tandem, with off-peak corrections that often exceeded 50% during the contraction cycles, as seen.

What’s Different This Time, and Final Thoughts

Now, the million-dollar, or billion-dollar, question, of course, is “Will it be different this time?” I’ve read my fair share of the bulls’ argument for a Yes answer. Eventually, their arguments boil down to this in my view: this time is different because 1) the AI-driven demand is secular and not cyclical, and 2) Micron has structurally shifted to long-term binding contracts. For example, judging by the recent ER, Micron has already sold out its entire 2026 HBM capacity under non-cancellable, fixed-price contracts. Unlike previous cycles where customers could walk away from spot-market orders, these binding agreements provide unprecedented revenue visibility.

However, I remain skeptical of both arguments. To counter the first point, since the beginning of modern computing, the demand for storage has been on a secular rise overall, driven by one technological breakthrough after another, such as the widespread use of PCs, laptops, superclusters, mobile computing, and also cloud computing. Overall, world storage capacity has expanded at a remarkable pace over the last several decades, maintaining a 26% compound annual growth rate, according to the historical data shown below. In 1986, the world storage size was a mere 2.6 exabytes, which grew to 6,800 exabytes in 2020. This is a growth of more than 2,600 times over about 40 years.

I think it’s fair to call it a “strong secular demand.” However, such a strong secular trend did not stop the sector from being highly cyclical in this period. I am not convinced of why the demand from AI this time can be “more secular” than what has happened in the past 40+ years.

As for the bulls’ second point, even the cause-effect logic is debatable in my view. My view is that MU is seeing more long-term contracts now because of the higher DRAM prices. In other words, I am not convinced at all that having more long-term contracts would cause higher DRAM prices and/or demand to persist. Experiences have taught me to trust human nature and profit seeking more than a disruptive technology or new contract structure. Customers accept long-term fixed-price contracts when they make better sense and can abandon them just as quickly when they stop making sense.

But of course, I cannot deny the fact that MU is currently benefiting tremendously from the combination of long-term contracts, robust demand, and favorable DRAM prices. It is an overpowering combination. As a reflection of its potency, Micron’s inventory has decreased significantly since reaching an extreme peak of over 200 days in early 2023 (see the next chart below). Thanks to the demand and long-term contracts, MU is maintaining a quite lean inventory of only about 123 days as of FQ2 2026, indicating better clarity of demand.

To close, this MU follow-up focused on the overall status of the current DRAM sector and MU’s updates reported in the FQ2 ER. These developments have led me to see large odds for MU to be near a cyclical peak. Judging by the updates provided in the ER, the company is certainly enjoying a combination of strong tailwinds, as detailed above.

However, I don’t agree with the bulls that these tailwinds have changed (or can change) the fundamental cyclical nature of the business. It is undeniable that these tailwinds are creating near-term benefits, but I believe these benefits are already priced in at its current price levels. These considerations have led me to a rating downgrade to Sell under current conditions.

data.title: Perrigo: Double-Digit Dividend At A Bargain Price
data.description:
My Investing Philosophy

I like dividend-paying stocks that have a history of raising the dividend consistently, year after year. I also like finding stocks that are currently out of favor, either due to macro events or temporary (but reversible) mistakes by management once they have learned the lesson and are likely to avoid making the same mistake again in the future. I believe today’s subject company, Perrigo (PRGO), fits that definition. With a 12% dividend yield and a P/E of 4.3, it looks like a bargain. So, I did some research to find out why it looks so tempting because many times a company’s yield is high and the P/E is low because it could be going out of business. But this is a stock I believe will rebound eventually, and a patient investor could reap outsized gains while collecting that rising dividend.

I also tend to hold stocks until the company changes its business model (unfavorably, in my opinion), cuts its dividend, or becomes ridiculously overvalued. In each case, I sell and look for a better option. Otherwise, I like to hold stocks forever and let the miracle of compounding and reinvesting dividends do most of the heavy lifting of growing my portfolio and income for me.

Company Background

Perrigo is an OTC (Over-The-Counter) maker of store-brand drugs, infant formula, and animal health products. It operates with two divisions: Consumer Self-Care Americas and Consumer Self-Care International. The company sells thousands of store-branded OTC products, with Walmart accounting for almost 13% of sales.

I like to find companies that have a competitive advantage within their respective industries. Perrigo is a low-cost leader, which enables it to be a valuable partner for discount retailers. Perrigo management tried to move upscale into a higher margin business and learned a difficult lesson. My expectation is that they will stick to what they know best and work on improving margins and volumes within their existing business and network of facilities, only adding small, bolt-on acquisitions that fit within the company’s core competencies.

“Perrigo is a scaled consumer health leader, ranked #1 in terms of volume in the U.S. and a top 10 player by dollar sales across the EU. So sensibly a U.S. and EU business. Our portfolio, as some of you will know, is highly diversified across 9 different categories with no single product contributing more than 5% of total net sales. So, it's a fairly risk-insulated model. We operate in about 30 countries with our commercial footprint, as I mentioned, centered in the U.S. and in Europe.

We deliver meaningful savings for consumers. The majority of our business, about 60% is in store brand offerings.” – Patrick Lockwood-Taylor, President, CEO & Director

The U.S. and EU healthcare systems save about $30B per year derived from generic offerings from Perrigo, according to the CEO in a recent conference presentation. This is a company that will not go under, even though it is priced as though it will.

Its roots go all the way back to 1887, when Luther Perrigo founded the company in Michigan and had the idea to package and distribute medicines and household items to country stores. The company moved its headquarters to Ireland in 2013.

Perrigo’s problems (share price is down 92% over the last ten years) began in 2013 with the acquisition of biotech company Elon for ~$7B, saddling the company with excessive debt. The company divested its prescription drug business in 2025 for $1.5B. The company is now focused on its core business of OTC store-branded products. I think this is good news, as management is now paying attention to improving margins and operational efficiencies rather than growth through acquisitions. I believe this will mark the bottom and a good turnaround point for the company. I also believe the company will be able to remain solvent and continue to raise its dividend, albeit at a much slower pace going forward.

Earnings

I expect that 2026 will be a foundational base year to build off of, given that management guidance forecasts EPS (earnings per share) to be flat to slightly down compared to 2025. Q4 revenue declined by 2%, while adjusted EPS rose by 7% but missed consensus estimates by $0.01. GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) EPS were negative due to a $1.3B non-cash goodwill impairment charge related to the divestitures in 2025.

There is a lot of seasonality to EPS for Perrigo, and when combined with the constant pressure from large retail chains trying to squeeze margins, predicting the future becomes complicated. Looking at the table below, we see a downward trend in both revenue and EPS. But some of that is due to divestitures (especially smaller ones that do not require restatement of financials). Those activities should be minimal in 2026 as management focuses on improving margins through applying operational efficiencies.

Q1 and Q2 of 2026 are expected to be the bottom for Perrigo earnings, with sequential improvements in Q3 & Q4 bringing EPS to a small increase in the final quarter to end the year. I am not sure the Q4 estimate will hold true, but analysts covering the company have EPS coming in at ~$2.11/share, while management is forecasting between $2.25 and $2.55.

Looking out further, I expect EPS to show improvement in 2027 and beyond, but not at a tremendous rate. This is a mature business with limited organic growth prospects except through either cost savings or acquisitions. It appears management is making cost savings their priority.

Growth Prospects

My expectation is for an average of 3% growth in EPS over the next five years, with 2026 forming the base from which to grow. The forward P/E ratio of 4.26 is extremely low, even for a mature company. Since the market is forward-looking, I expect the future earnings growth will begin to have a positive impact on the share price as early as Q2 of this year.

Management expects its Three-S Plan (Stabilize, Streamline, Strengthen) to generate annual cost savings of $80M to $100M per year once fully implemented during 2026. The one-time cost, estimated at $80M to $90M, will weigh on 2026 EPS and margins but is expected to begin to bear fruit in the second half of the year and more fully in 2027 and beyond. Controlling costs is what is core to the company’s culture and strategy. It strayed from that temporarily, learned a valuable lesson, and is now getting back to what it does best.

Profitability

This is a relatively low-margin business, but Perrigo has operated in this environment for most of its existence, and other than the problems that arose from its misguided attempt to stray away from its core, management has consistently generated free cash flow and remained profitable. The profitability grade shown below shows us that Perrigo’s EBIT and EBITDA margins are superior to the sector average, meaning it is not likely to go out of business anytime soon.

Balance Sheet

The balance sheet is improving and provides encouragement for investors with a long-term horizon. Management continues to be committed to reducing debt and increasing the dividend and plans to use proceeds from divestitures to reduce debt. The second table below details how much debt has been repaid over the last several years. I like the consistent effort by management.

Other product lines are under review, which could lead to another divestiture or two in 2026 or 2027, and I would expect any such actions to further strengthen the balance sheet.

Dividend

This is the most exciting part for rising income investors like myself. The current yield is 12%, and the payout ratio of 42% leaves ample room for future increases. Increases have averaged 5.2% over the past five years, but I expect the rate going forward will be closer to 3% to match my expectations for overall EPS growth.

My long-term inflation expectation is for an average of 2% to 2.5%, so as long as the dividend increases are able to keep up with that pace, I’ll be happy with my 12.5% yield on my initial investment.

Valuation

I will value the company using two methods. First, I will use the DDM (Dividend Discount Model), and then I will use a method assuming reversion to the mean on a multiple valuation basis. So, the DDM formula looks like this:

(D/(M-X)) = V

Where:

V = Fair Value

D = Current Dividend

M = Discount rate (I use my required rate of return on investment)

X = Expected future average annual increase in dividend

(1.16/(.09-.03)= $19.33

Perrigo is in a slow growth, mature industry with relatively low margins, so my required return is only 9%, the current dividend is $1.16, and the expected growth in the dividend is 3%. When we plug these numbers into the model, the result is $19.33 per share, slightly more than double the current share price of about $9.60. This indicates that the price is a bargain, selling at a discount to fair value of 50.3%. I believe that the price will achieve its fair value within the next five years, so my expected annual compound average price gain will be ~15%, calculated in Excel as follows:

=((19.33/9.60)^(1/5))-1

If we add the 15% annual valuation gain to the dividend yield of 12% and the expected EPS growth rate of 3%, we end up with an expected compound annual average total return of 30.1%, which is probably a stretch. Even if the stock price only achieves half of the valuation gain I project, it would still generate a compound average annual total return of 23%.

Now I want to look at the value from a multiple perspective, assuming that the P/E ratio will revert to its historical mean over the next five years. The 10-year average P/E for PRGO is 15. But with a lower growth rate over the next five years, I expect a more appropriate expectation for the P/E is 9. The current P/E is 4.3, so the stock is trading at a discount of 52.4% of fair value. Fair value is estimated to be $20.16. So, if we calculate the compound average annual increase from a multiple expansion, we get 14.4%. The calculation in Excel is as follows:

=((9/4.3)^(1/5))-1

When we add to the annual expected valuation increase of 14.4%, the current dividend yield of 12%, and the expected EPS growth rate of 3%, we find the expected compound annual total return of 29.4%. That result is very close to the DDM estimate, and either one would be very exciting, especially from a mature OTC products company.

One more way to look at the valuation is to consider how much we would collect from dividends over the next five years plus a projected price based upon a P/E of 9 by the end of the term. If the dividend increases by 3% per year, we can expect to collect ~$6.16 in dividends per share on our initial investment of $9.64. Even if the stock does not appreciate at all, we would have recouped nearly 64% of our initial investment just in dividends. Now, if we assume EPS will grow at 3% per year with forward EPS for 2026 estimated at $2.24/share, EPS in 2030 should be $2.44/share. Multiply that by an expected P/E of 9, and we get an expected share price of $22.03. The gain from $9.64 to $22.03 is $12.43; add the cumulated dividends for a total return of $18.58, or 193% over five years.

Again, it may not achieve the full reversion to the mean I expect, but I can wait a little longer as long as I keep collecting a 12% dividend. My goal is to at least double my initial investment over the next five years. To achieve that, I need the P/E to rise only to 5.36 while EPS grows at only 3%. I really think that is very attainable.

Risks

There are two risks that I foresee. First is that the environment of slow demand for store branded, OTC products continues for many years instead of normalizing. This would be more likely in a booming economy with strong jobs growth. I think we could have decent growth, but with AI (artificial intelligence) eliminating some jobs, it may be presumptuous to expect strong jobs growth over the next five years. A weak employment environment should lead to more consumers opting for the lower cost alternative of OTC, store-branded products over the higher priced name-brand items. So, my assessment of this risk is that the probability is fairly low. But things can change if the war in Iran escalates further and lasts longer than I anticipate.

The second risk is that management forgets its lessons and makes another bad acquisition or that it is unable to generate the expected savings from its Three-S project. Again, I doubt either is likely, but it is possible. No one can see the future, and there could be changes in management personnel bringing with it a new plan.

Either way, I still think the risk is relatively low due to Perrigo’s relations with its customers and its broad product base (over 1,000 products with no one product accounting for more than 5% of revenue).

Conclusion

Perrigo has been beaten down as though it is about to go bankrupt with a P/E of 4.3. The dividend appears safe and, at 12%, is juicy enough for me to buy and hold for the turnaround. I rate Perrigo a Buy for patient investors desiring current income along with eventual appreciation potential.

data.title: Velo3D: Turnaround Goal Just Pushed Further Out, Investors Are Not Happy
data.description:
Introduction

Velo3D, Inc. (VELO) caught my eye because of the massive move after they reported their full-year 2025 results, which showed massive margin deterioration as well as a delay in profitability. I wanted to take a look at the company’s supposed turnaround in more detail and give some comments on why there is still a lot of work to be done.

Briefly on the Company

Velo3D is in the business of making 3D printing systems and related software that allow customers to essentially build custom parts for their needs, as long as the blueprints fit the dimensions of the printer. These printers allow the manufacturing of complex metal parts used in aerospace, defense, and other industrial segments, with the core value proposition being that they let engineers print parts with less compromise and with less support material. Most of the revenue still comes from the sale of its Sapphire printers, but some positive shifts allow for other revenue streams to come through, specifically the Rapid Production solutions, or RPS, which essentially involves parts production, allowing customers to develop applications, and then they can produce the parts in-house or have VELO manufacture them.

By the Numbers

Looking at the headline numbers, it does not look particularly good. Revenue came in at $9.4m, which is down over 25% y/y. We can look at the breakdown in sales in more detail to see that the Support Services segment saw a particularly bad quarter, declining by over 62% to $1.7m from $4.5m. The main revenue generator, which is its 3D printers and parts, also didn’t perform as well as investors would have liked. It is down around 5% y/y to around $7.6m. Now, the following part is where it gets even worse. Q4 GAAP EPS came in at a loss of $1.03, while adjusted net loss per share was a little better at -$0.54. GAAP gross margin was deep in the red, at around -74%, which is a rarity. This anomaly is driven by around $7m write-off of obsolete inventory and production volume delays that stemmed from the government shutdown in Q4 of 2025. So, I’m guessing the company will see an improvement in the next quarter, unless there are more one-time write-offs.

For the full year, sales ended up at $46m, which is a slight improvement on last year’s $41m. The 3D printer and parts segment saw a growth of 54% y/y, which doesn’t seem to be reflected in the headline numbers because the recurring payments segment went to essentially zero, and support services declined 35%, as well as the “Other” revenue segment was down 89% from $5m to $524K. Margins across the board are, of course, still very much negative, but are slowly improving. For example, adjusted EBITDA went from -$58.5m to -$33.3m. But since there was that inventory write-off this quarter, gross margins for the full year deteriorated further into the red, going from -5.1% to -16.1%. Unfortunately, the company does not have the consistency to produce enough gross profit to cover all its operating costs, which is honestly a little worrying.

In such situations, the financial position is very important, so let’s take a look at VELO’s balance sheet. VELO finished the year with $39m in cash and equivalents, which is a massive improvement over last year’s $1.2m due to a successful offering. The company still has around $30m in total debt. This is not the most current picture of the debt since a couple of weeks ago, the company managed to convert 60% of it to shares, which leaves around $12m of debt. That is still a lot of debt for a company that isn’t able to cover its gross costs. For now, the cash on hand covers it, and the annual interest expense will eat up around $1.6m a year after conversion if they stay at this level, and its cash from operations doesn’t cover any of it either, as it is deep in the red, too. CFO improved to -$27.6m from -$32.6m, but it is still not great.

The company burns around $2.3m in cash every month. It cannot fund itself, so it needs all the help from outside sources it can get, and that will mean further share offerings or debt refinancing to keep afloat. At this rate, if nothing changes, it can survive a little over a year with that cash pile, but that doesn’t take into account the debt outstanding, which either will need to be repaid or converted to shares as well.

So, on top of these results, the management provided guidance for 2026. Revenue is expected to be around $65m at the midpoint, which is above analysts’ expectations of around $63m. Capex is going to be around $45m for the year, too, so still no positive cash flow. It seems that the company is in its aggressive investment phase, and I view it as positive, but I also hope that it is not in vain. Additionally, the management expects to see gross margins returning to positive territory, to around 30%, which is a massive improvement. I think the big kicker of today’s disastrous open, which saw its share price plummet around 25% as of writing this update, is the fact that the management pushed its EBITDA breakeven to the second half of 2026. The delay in profitability pushed a lot of people over the edge, and many looked like they threw in the towel, as hopes for a quick turnaround seemed to have worsened.

Overall, the report was not particularly great in all honesty. There were some glimmers of hope, but operations are far from even turning breakeven, so the hopes of profitability are not even on the table right now. The delay in profitability was the final nail in the coffin for many hopeful investors.

Comments on the Outlook

The narrative right now is around defense contracts and the ability for the company to secure wins in order to become profitable, or even to just not go bankrupt. Currently, the company has secured a decent-sized contract with the Department of War tied to the Project FORGE, and a $11.5m multi-year production contract for a sensitive national-security program. These contracts helped the company’s backlog improve to around $31m, but it is still not quite enough to make a massive difference in the company’s operations.

I wouldn’t call defense the saving grace for the company, but certainly, it is a big part of the future outlook for VELO. But you see, we investors like narrative, and it is easy to cling to a specific narrative like defense, because it is the U.S. government, and it has deep pockets. As long as VELO can demonstrate its value proposition and win more awards in the future, I can see the company turning itself around successfully. Execution and management of resources are key in this situation because if one fails, I don’t think there is an easy way out besides a massive restructuring, which essentially means bankruptcy.

To succeed, the company needs to focus not only on government defense contracts but also on other end-markets, which are commercial aerospace and production services. If the company can successfully navigate this critical moment and keep winning contracts, there is hope for growth still, but as I said earlier, the time is running out. There’s not much mentioned regarding the “going concern” topic in its filings; for example, there’s no wording for “substantial doubt” or similar phrasing when it comes to continuing operations, but I wouldn’t be surprised if this appears sometime in the future, if operations do not materially improve. The only phrasing I found in its latest 8K is in the Forward-looking statements part, where it says that the company might not have the ability to continue as a going concern.

I don’t think they are too worried about that, at least not in the public eye, since they do plan to build additional capacity of approximately 400 production systems over the next decade. It could be just a carrot dangling in front of willing investors who would like to provide them with the additional capital necessary to keep surviving and become a self-fulfilling prophecy.

Closing Comments

VELO seems to be too risky at the moment for me, but I will be following its story along, as I believe there might be a way out for everyone in the future, whether that may be a buyout or a successful turnaround, which will unlock the full potential of the operations. For now, however, the management has a lot of work ahead of them to change my mind and prove that the company is not slowly heading toward bankruptcy filings and restructuring.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
